coredata.dc:title: An environment-friendly route to explore the carbon quantum dots derived from curry berries (Murrayakoenigii L) as a fluorescent biosensor for detecting vitamin B12
coredata.dc:creator: Preethi, M.
Viswanathan, C.
Ponpandian, N.
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in bio/analytic research are an exciting fluorescence method due to their peculiar characteristics. Herewith, the novelty of our present work is the green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs through ultrasonication treatment and developed for sensitive and selective detection towards cobalamin (vitamin B12). To our knowledge, for the first time, we synthesized the CQDs from the curry berries (Murrayakoenigii) by simple ultrasonication treatment. The crystal structure, functional groups, morphology, and photoluminescence properties were explored. The main element of Vitamin B12 is Co2+, which is related to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Under certain conditions, the fluorescent intensity was gradually decreased with the concentration of cobalamin in the different concentrations of 0–0.40 μM, as well as the limit of detection, which was 0.04 μM, which shows that the synthesized CQDs can be used effectively as a vitamin B12 sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green extraction of CQDS
Curryberries
Ultrasonication technique
Detection of cobalamin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for synthesis of the CQDs
Selectivity and sensitivity towards vitamin B12
Interaction of CQDs with Co2+ ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the DST-FIST; DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities in the department.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of monodispersed CQDs (inset) SAED pattern, (b) The particle size distribution, (c) XRD pattern, (d) FTIR spectrum, (e) Raman spectrum and (f) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of CQDs of various wavelengths from 320 to 460 nm. (b) Effect of pH on PL, (c) the long-term stability of CQDs synthesized over 30 days, (d) The stability of CQDs within the existence of varying different vitamins, (e) detection of vitamin B12 at different concentrations from 0 to 0.40 μM and (f) calibration graph for CQDs with a linear relationship of 0.997.
Co-ordination interaction for the formation of CQD with Co2+ ions.
Recovery of Vitamin B12 in milk powder (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An environment-friendly route to explore the carbon quantum dots derived from curry berries (Murrayakoenigii L) as a fluorescent biosensor for detecting vitamin B12

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in bio/analytic research are an exciting fluorescence method due to their peculiar characteristics. Herewith, the novelty of our present work is the green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs through ultrasonication treatment and developed for sensitive and selective detection towards cobalamin (vitamin B12). To our knowledge, for the first time, we synthesized the CQDs from the curry berries (Murrayakoenigii) by simple ultrasonication treatment. The crystal structure, functional groups, morphology, and photoluminescence properties were explored. The main element of Vitamin B12 is Co2+, which is related to the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Under certain conditions, the fluorescent intensity was gradually decreased with the concentration of cobalamin in the different concentrations of 0–0.40 μM, as well as the limit of detection, which was 0.04 μM, which shows that the synthesized CQDs can be used effectively as a vitamin B12 sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B12, a complex containing cobalt ions, which are necessary for a biological DNA synthesis, and the metabolic pathway is associated with the formation of the bone marrow erythrocytes, the development in red blood cells (RBCs), and the retention of a nervous system. Children may show signs of unresponsive, mental disorders, anemia, and other symptoms because of deficiency in Vitamin B12 [1]. It is undoubtedly important for building a highly selective and sensitive analytical technique for detecting the presence of vitamin B12.

Nowadays, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been developed for sensing applications because of their remarkable properties such as low toxicity, wavelength-tunable photoluminescence emission, outstanding biocompatibility, water-solubility, easy functionalization, and high stability [2]. Various technologies have already been established to accomplish the synthesis of CQDs, but among these ultrasonication treatment continues to attract attention due to their low cost, easy, simple, and environment-friendly nature, and also the availability of natural carbon. In this research work, we picked curry berries as a source of carbon for the synthesis of CQDs via ultrasonication treatment. To our knowledge, no studies have been reported concerning the production of CQDs from curry berries as well as the detection of Vitamin B12 using photoluminescence quenching.

Curry berries were collected from our backyard garden (Coimbatore, Tamilnadu, India). All of the chemicals used for this analysis were from an analytical grade of Sigma-Aldrich and Himedia chemicals and were used without purification. Deionized water (DI) was used to synthesize all aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for synthesis of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were extracted through curryberries using a one-step ultrasonication technique and its schematic representation (Fig. S1). The 30 mL of the curryberries juice was blended with 25 mL of distilled water using a domestic mixer. The precipitate was extracted using Whatman filter paper from curry berries. Then, the obtained solution was sonicated and centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 rpm to extract the larger particles. The resultant solution was purified with a pore size of 0.2 μm using the Whatman filter paper and dried to obtain CQDs. The CQDs dispersion was used to analyze the sensitivity of different vitamins (S2).

X-ray diffraction patterns were measured using an X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku Smart Lab) system. The HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100) measured the particle size as well as the morphology of the CQDs. The Raman spectrum was measured using Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab Ram-HR. The FTIR spectra were measured using a Bruker Tensor FT-IR spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was measured by Fluoromax-4, a spectrofluorometer.

The HR-TEM confirmed that the carbon quantum dots were spherical and ranged from 2 to 8 nm (Fig. 1 (a)). The amorphous structure of CQDs was characterized using the SAED pattern (Fig. 1(a)inset). The size distribution for CQDs was calculated using ImageJ software in Fig. 1(b). These observations indicate that the CQDs are amorphous carbon lattices.

X-ray diffraction has investigated the molecular structure of the CQDs (Fig. 1(c)). These diffraction peaks appear in the CQDs are 14.44° (d001 = 0.611 nm), 29.44° (d002 = 0.3 nm), 39.53°, and 47.89° correspond to an amorphous carbon phase and partial graphitization of CQDs [3]. A phenomenon where trace impurities and/or faults generate detectable noiseand this causes an overall lower signal-to-noise ratio, poor crystallinity, and reduced intensity peaks [4]. The CQDs were discovered to be abundant and had a poorcrystalline structure, as in previous reports [5].

The different functional groups and the interaction of CQDS were then investigated using an FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(d)). The peaks at 3300 cm−1 (O–H), 2110 cm−1 (CC), 1640 cm−1 (CC), 1167 cm−1 (C–H), 1044 cm−1 (C–O–C) and 667 cm−1 (C–H) [6]. The fact that these functional groups exist shows that both the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups demonstrate the remarkable water solubility of synthesized carbon quantum dots from curryberries.

A characteristic D (disorder) band of 1370 cm−1 shown in the Raman spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 1(e)) attributes to structural defects and a G (graphite) band with a variation of 1590 cm−1 attributed by the first-order scattering vibration mode E2g on the graphite sheet [7]. The intensity ratio (ID / IG), showing the defect concentration, about 0.85. This variable indicates that the CQDs have a high number of defects.

UV–visible absorption studies were conducted to examine the optical properties of CQDS (Fig. 1(f)). CQDS observed absorption peaks in the UV region at 276 nm, 325 nm, and 385 nm due to the π-π * and n-π * transition, and these findings are well associated with the other reported studies [8,9].

The photoluminescence spectrum shows its emission wavelength for CQDs approaches a high PL intensity at 510 nm when using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). The quantum yield for CQDs has 10.24% at a wavelength of about 360 nm. The PL intensity was constant under neutral pH conditions and the CQDs were influenced by acidic and basic conditions. (Fig. 2(b)). CQDs retained 90% of their PL intensity and no significant change was seen when freshly prepared over 30 days (Fig. 2(c)). In addition, the CQDs were demonstrated to be a good probe for vitamin B12 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity towards vitamin B12
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the prepared CQDs dispersion was tested by experimenting with vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12) which act as chelators with the concentration of 0–0.40 μM (Fig. 2(d)). By examining the graph, we found that vitamin B12 showed its minimal value of (F/F0). The CQDs samples were prepared by adding vitamin B12 at different concentrations originating from 0 to 0.40 μM (Fig. 2(e)). The PL intensity decreases with an increase in the concentration of vitamin B12. We tried to compare the performance and efficiency of the proposed method of CQD from different carbon sources with the existing detection of the Vitamin B12 sensor (Table S3).

This graph assumes the linear relationship between both the relative intensity [(F0 − F)/F] with various concentrations ofCQDs and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2(f)). The limit of detection for vitamin B12 was calculated as 0.04 μM. Similarly, these are additionally verified by this Stern-Volmer plot with an ideal correlation coefficient for 0.997 within the variations of vitamin B12 concentrations.

The CQDs were used to demonstrate the real sample analysis (milk powder) for the detection of vitamin B12. The recovery of real sample analysis was studied using the standard addition method in the three concentrations of 0.10 µM (low), 0.20 µM (medium), and 0.30 µM (high) at a pH of 7. Each sample was measured three times under the same conditions (n = 3). The recovery results are displayed (Table 1 ) and range from 93 to 97.6%, suggesting that the proposed approach is more viable for vitamin B12 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of CQDs with Co2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic representation of CQDs with Co2+ ions (Fig. 3 ). Vitamin B12 is mostly composed of Co2+, which is involved in the quenching of fluorescence by CQDs. Owing to the strong interaction between CQDs and Co2+ ions, the PL strength of the CQDs decreases with increases in the concentration of vitamin B12. These CQDs surface energy or electron transfer may be attributed to Co2+ ion's ability to quench fluorescence. Certain oxygen-containing groups occur on the surface of CQDs that can form complexes with Co2+, resulting in the transition of electrons from CQDs to Co2+. As the CQD and Co2+ ions complexes form, the fluorescence of the CQDs is significantly diminished [10].

In summary, the simplified, cheap, fast, and green synthesis method was successfully demonstrated, with the development of green fluorescent CQDs using an ultrasonication technique making use of curry berries as a carbon source. The fluorescence quench effect of the CQD in the presence of vitamin B12 provides a tool for detecting vitamin B12 in an aqueous solution with a LOD of 0.04 μM. Additionally,CQDs that have been synthesized could also be effective for vitamin B12 label-free detection.

M. Preethi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. C. Viswanathan: Validation, Formal analysis. N. Ponpandian: Conceptualization, Validation, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the DST-FIST; DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities in the department.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.130521.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
